title: Wireless Throughput Guide
summary: Understand SNR, channel width, shared clients, utilization factor, and realistic wireless throughput expectations.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates practical wireless throughput from signal quality, channel width, client sharing, and usable airtime assumptions.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document wireless design assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch RF, capacity, or margin pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when estimating realistic Wi-Fi performance, explaining why PHY rate is not usable throughput, or validating capacity assumptions before AP count is finalized.
bullets: Early wireless design planning.
Expansion review or refresh planning.
Existing-system cleanup, validation, or troubleshooting support.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each input represents in practical wireless planning terms.

items.label: SNR (dB)
items.description: The signal-to-noise ratio available for the wireless connection.
items.whyItMatters: SNR strongly affects modulation rate, retries, and throughput stability.
items.commonMistake: Using channel width or AP generation as the only throughput input.

links.label: Calculate SNR from signal and noise

items.label: Channel Width (MHz)
items.description: The RF channel width used for the wireless connection.
items.whyItMatters: Wider channels may raise peak throughput but can increase overlap and reduce channel reuse.
items.commonMistake: Using wide channels in crowded areas without checking overlap.

items.label: Clients Sharing AP
items.description: The number of clients competing for the AP’s airtime.
items.whyItMatters: Wireless throughput is shared, so more active clients reduce per-client experience.
items.commonMistake: Dividing by associated clients without considering which clients are active.

items.label: Utilization Factor (%)
items.description: The percentage of theoretical throughput expected to be usable after airtime, overhead, retries, and real-world conditions.
items.whyItMatters: This converts ideal link behavior into a more realistic usable throughput estimate.
items.commonMistake: Assuming ideal PHY rates translate directly into application throughput.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means throughput assumptions appear practical. Watch means SNR, channel width, or client sharing should be reviewed. Risk means actual user throughput may fall short unless RF conditions or capacity are improved.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but RF conditions, client behavior, or operating margin should be reviewed.
Risk: add capacity, reduce assumptions, improve placement, or validate with a survey before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Wireless planning problems usually come from treating Wi-Fi like a fixed cable instead of a shared RF environment affected by airtime, interference, device capability, channel reuse, and roaming behavior.
bullets: Sizing only for coverage and ignoring capacity.
Using ideal vendor rates as if they are sustained usable throughput.
Ignoring noise floor, channel overlap, client mix, and airtime contention.
Failing to document assumptions before AP count, channel plan, or antenna placement is finalized.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace a wireless survey, manufacturer documentation, controller validation, spectrum analysis, or project-specific RF engineering judgment.
bullets: Vendor specifications and controller guidance still matter.
Client device capability can be the limiting factor even when AP hardware is strong.
Walls, mounting height, interference, antenna pattern, and channel reuse can change the final result.
A wireless design with margin is usually easier to support, tune, and expand.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal wireless design review, site survey validation, manufacturer guidance, or project-specific responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual AP model, antenna pattern, client mix, controller settings, and site survey results.
Confirm stakeholder expectations for coverage, speed, roaming, and reliability.
Document assumptions before implementation or final AP placement.
